title: Fallen Land: A Post-Apocalyptic Board Game - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pUPzkqLt5WM

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens with a long, fond detour through Arthur's Toys — the dingy, wonderful Fresno hobby shop of his childhood, where Steve Jackson games came in Ziploc baggies and you suspected you might be buying the only copy in existence. **Fallen Land**, recommended by a viewer and bought blind on Amazon, is the first board game in years to give him exactly that DIY-mecca feeling.

At its core, Fallen Land is *post-apocalyptic Talisman*: roll-and-move across a hex map of a wrecked America, draw a random encounter, run skill checks against the threat. But the surface comparison undersells the depth. Each player runs one of ten factions (the Brotherhood, the Highwaymen, the Sons of Neptune…) with a distinct starting town, faction perks, and a small piece of well-written backstory on the back of every sheet. Parties are six characters and a vehicle, equipped from a deck of *hundreds* of unique loot cards — gold bars, a wrist rocket, an indestructible tennis racquet, the Mystery Machine, a copy of Fallen Land itself.

Two big surprises: the writing is genuinely good (the rulebook is forty pages of well-organised, indexed, illustrated guidance), and the competitive mode is *vicious* — a whole second deck of red-dot "take that" action cards including booby traps, hostage-taking, and Montezuma's Revenge for the opposing town. The skill-check system is a 10s-and-remainders system that takes some wrapping-around but rewards the mathy crowd.

----

Daniel frames Fallen Land as a love-it-or-hate-it game whose amateur-charm art and table-hogging fiddliness will alienate exactly the players who don't recognise themselves in his Arthur's Toys story. Is that *aesthetic ancestry* — DIY indie roots, photocopied rulebooks, kitchen-table art — something you actively look for in a game, or actively avoid?
